1: Standard prophetic-word formula opens the oracle against false prophets. The formal structure — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor — is followed immediately by the direct address ben-ʾadam (son of man), Ezekiel's consistent designation that marks his humanity before the divine commission.
2: hinave ʾel-neviʾei Yisraʾel hannivveʾim — "prophesy against the prophets of Israel who are prophesying" — the piel imperative hinave followed by the niphal participle hannivveʾim creates an ironic opposition: Ezekiel who truly prophesies (piel) is commanded to address those who claim to prophesy (niphal participial, merely performing the act). The phrase haholkhim ʾaḥar ruḥam — "who follow their own spirit" — names the source: not the divine ruaḥ but their own.
3: hoi ʾal-neviʾei hannavalim ʾasher holkhim ʾaḥar ruḥam — the hoi (woe) oracle begins. Navalim is the word for "foolish/wicked" (related to naval, the wicked man of 1 Sam 25 and Psalms 14:1). The characterization emphasizes moral rather than merely intellectual failure. Umebal lo raʾu — "and have seen nothing" — the false prophets are blind to the divine reality they claim to disclose.
4: keshvaʿalim baḥaravot neviʾeikha Yisraʾel hayu — "your prophets, O Israel, have been like jackals among ruins" — the jackal-in-ruins image is a desolation metaphor: jackals inhabit abandoned places (Ezekiel 14:15; Isaiah 34:13; Jeremiah 9:11). The false prophets inhabit the ruin of the covenant community rather than building it up or standing in the breach (v5).
5: loʾ ʿalitem bifretsoyt vattivnu gader ʾal-beit Yisraʾel laʿamod bammiilḥama beyom YHWH — "you have not gone up into the breaches nor built up a wall for the house of Israel to stand in battle on the day of YHWH" — the wall/breach metaphor (paratsot, breaches; gader, wall) is the defining image of prophetic failure. True prophets stand in the breach (intercede in the gap between YHWH's anger and the people); false prophets have abandoned this intercessory function.
6: ḥazu shav veqesem kazav haʾomrim neʾum YHWH vaYHWH loʾ shelaḥam — "they have seen false vision and lying divination, saying "Thus says YHWH," when YHWH has not sent them" — the term shav (false/vain) is the same word used in the third commandment ("do not take the name of YHWH in vain"). Using the prophetic formula without divine commission is precisely the violation of that commandment.
7: The series of rhetorical questions — halo ḥazon shav ḥazitem umigesem kazav ʾamartem veʾomrim neʾum YHWH vaʾani loʾ dibbartim — "have you not seen a false vision and spoken a lying divination, when you say "Thus says YHWH," but I have not spoken?" — the ʾani loʾ dibbartim (but I have not spoken) is the decisive divine refutation. The first-person denial of authorship removes all legitimacy from their messages.
8: The result clause — lakhein koh ʾamar ʾAdanai YHWH yaʾan dabberkem shav vaḥazitem kazav lakhein ḥinneni ʾaleikhem — "therefore thus says Lord YHWH: because you have spoken falsehood and seen lies, therefore I am against you" — the double lakhein (therefore... therefore) creates a formal legal structure: accusation → verdict. Hinneni ʾaleikhem (behold I am against you) is the opposite of ʾani ittekhem (I am with you) — the divine hostile-presence formula.
9: vehayetah yadi ʾel-hanneviʾim haḥozim shav vehaqqosemim kazav besod ʿammi loʾ yihyu — the three-part exclusion: (1) besod ʿammi loʾ yihyu (they will not be in the council/assembly of my people); (2) bikhethav beit Yisraʾel loʾ yikkathvu (in the register of the house of Israel they will not be written); (3) veʾel-ʾadmat Yisraʾel loʾ yavoʾu (to the land of Israel they will not come). The exclusions move from community to register to land — a comprehensive disinheritance.
10: yaʿan uveyaʿan hittu ʾet-ʿammi leʾmor shalom veʾein shalom vehu bone ḥayits vehinnam totim ʾoto tafeil — "because they have misled my people, saying peace when there is no peace; and when the people build a wall, they plaster it with whitewash" — the peace/no-peace (shalom veʾein shalom) contrast echoes Jeremiah 6:14 and Ezekiel 8:11. The whitewash word tafeil (plaster/smear) is the key technical term — applying a cosmetic covering to an inadequate structure.
11: The coming storm that will collapse the plastered wall — shotef geshem vehavi ʾavnei ʾelgavish tappol veruaḥ seʿarot tevaqeʿa — "the flooding rain will fall, hailstones will fall, and a stormy wind will break out" — the storm as YHWH's judgment uses meteorological imagery for historical events (the Babylonian invasion). The three agents — rain (geshem shotef), hail (ʾavnei ʾelgavish), wind (ruaḥ seʿarot) — are covenant-curse weapons (cf. Exodus 9:22-26).
12: The rhetorical question that exposes the whitewash failure — heʾayye hattiḥ ʾasher taḥtem — "where is the plaster with which you plastered it?" — anticipates the community asking where the false prophets' message went when the wall collapsed. The question is directed by the survivors to the prophets who gave false assurance. The tafal/tiḥ terms (tiḥ is more general, tafal more specific) may represent dialectal variation or near-synonyms.
13: YHWH announces the storm personally — uvʾappe aʾaseh ruaḥ seʿarot (in my wrath I will make a stormy wind) — the divine anger (ʾap, nose/anger) is the source of the meteorological weapons. The personal agency (aʾaseh, I will make/cause) makes explicit what the false prophets denied: divine action is real and imminent.
14: The wall pulled down and its foundation laid bare — veḥarastiv ʾel-haʾarets venighla yesodo — uses the same verb haras (pull down/demolish) that appears in Ezekiel's commission (Ezekiel 5:16: breaking the staff of bread). The foundation exposure is the decisive act: not just superficial damage but structural collapse.
15: The recognition formula follows (veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH) immediately after the destruction, as it does throughout Ezekiel. Here it is applied specifically to the false prophets: even they will know YHWH's identity when their message is falsified by events.
16: Summary statement restating the core accusation — the prophets of Israel who prophesied to Jerusalem and saw visions of peace for her (ḥozim lah ḥazon shalom veʾein shalom) — "when there is no peace." The repetition of shalom veʾein shalom brackets the entire oracle against false prophets (vv3-16), making the peace-lie the defining sin of false prophecy.
17: The second oracle unit addresses the prophetesses — ven-ʾadam sim panekha ʾel-benot ʿammekha hannivveʾot — "son of man, set your face against the daughters of your people who are prophesying" — the gendered shift is grammatically marked: benot (daughters) vs. neviʾei (male prophets). Their sin is outlined as involving specific objects and techniques not fully understood.
18: kesatot ʿal kol-mafrqot yadeyhem — "who sew magic bands on all wrists" — kesatot (plural of kesat) are unknown objects, possibly wristbands or cushions used in divination; the term occurs only here in the OT. The mispakhot (veils/coverings) are also uncertain — possibly used to cover faces in entrancement or as part of divination rituals. The exact practice is opaque but the condemnation of it as false prophecy is clear.
19: The prostituting of divine service for handfuls of barley and scraps of bread — vattekhallelenah ʾoti ʾel-ʿammi bishʾele shiʿorei seʿorim uvifitetei leḥem — the trivialization of the prophetic office for minimal payment is the economic dimension of their false prophecy. ḥillel (piel: profane/desecrate) is the same root as ḥalal (pierce/profane); the piel makes it intensive — utterly profaning the divine name among the people.
20: YHWH tearing the bands from the arms — hinneni ʾel-kesatoteikhem ʾasher ʾattem mefarshot sham ʾet-hannefattot — the hostile-presence formula (hinneni ʾel) applied to the implements of false prophecy. The liberation of the souls (implied: those who were ensnared by the false prophetesses) is the positive dimension of the judgment on the practitioners.
21: The veils torn — veqaraʿti ʾet-mispekhoteykem vehtsal ʾet-ʿammi miyyadekhem — the tearing verb qaraʿ (tear/rend) is used for the tearing of garments in mourning and for divine judgment (1 Samuel 15:27-28; 1 Kings 11:11). The imagery parallels the wall-plaster removal of vv11-14: false protective coverings torn away.
22: The moral indictment — bifashovtekhem lev-tsaddiq sheqer vaʾani loʾ hikhʾavtihu — "because you disheartened the righteous with lies when I had not grieved him" — the verb khiʾavtihu (caused him grief) is from kaaʾav (to be in pain). YHWH had not intended to pain the righteous; the false prophetesses did so independently. The counterpart is strengthening the wicked by their false assurances.
23: The future cessation — lakhein shav loʾ tḥazena uqesem loʾ teqsomna ʿod — imperfects of permanent refusal: "you will no longer see false visions nor practice divination." The definitive verb hitsalti ʾet-ʿammi miyyadekhem (I will rescue my people from your hand) uses the rescue vocabulary (natsal, hiphil) normally applied to deliverance from enemies. The false prophetesses are treating YHWH's people as prey.
